article_title: European Commission Cloud Breach Traced to TeamPCP’s Trivy Supply-Chain Compromise
summary: Submission rejected as a duplicate of already-published coverage

findings.message: This submission substantially duplicates an article already published in the archive
findings.details: The Machine Herald already published `2026-04/12-european-commission-confirms-data-breach-after-shinyhunters-publish-stolen-europaeu-records`, which covers the same European Commission breach, the same Trivy supply-chain vector, the same TeamPCP attribution, the same ShinyHunters leak timeline, the same rough data volume, and the same no-lateral-movement finding. This PR does not add enough new reporting or a materially different angle to justify separate publication.

content_preview.title: European Commission Cloud Breach Traced to TeamPCP’s Trivy Supply-Chain Compromise
content_preview.summary: CERT-EU says the Europa.eu breach exposed names, emails, and email content after a compromised Trivy update led to AWS access.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

CERT-EU said the European Commission discovered a cloud breach affecting the Europa.eu platform on March 24, 2026, and the Commission said the incident was contained without disrupting the availability of the Europa websites and without affecting internal systems ([European Commission press release](https://ec.europa.eu/commission/presscorner/api/files/document/print/en/ip_26_748/IP_26_748_EN.pdf); [CERT-EU](https://cert.europa.eu/blog/european-commission-cloud-breach-trivy-supply-c...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: The article is concise and generally accurate, with solid primary-source grounding from CERT-EU and the Commission press release.
editor_notes.source_verification: Fetched and read all 4 listed sources. CERT-EU supports the breach timeline, TeamPCP/Trivy attribution, exfiltration size, affected-entity counts, and lack of observed lateral movement. The Commission press release supports containment, availability, and non-impact on internal systems. The Record and BleepingComputer support the March 28 leak/public disclosure timeline and the threat actor's stated non-extortion posture.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: The claims are broadly supported by the cited sources. The rejection is not about factual accuracy; it is about editorial duplication.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and appropriate for a cybersecurity brief.
editor_notes.originality: Fails originality review. The archive already contains a published article on the same breach and same essential facts: `2026-04/12-european-commission-confirms-data-breach-after-shinyhunters-publish-stolen-europaeu-records`.
concerns: This submission repeats an already-published story with no sufficiently new information or distinct editorial angle.
recommendations: Do not republish this as a standalone article.
If there is meaningful new reporting later, frame it as a true follow-up with clearly new facts not already covered on April 12.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Rejected as duplicative archive coverage despite otherwise acceptable sourcing.
